metadata.title: Why Greedy Can Guarantee Half the Optimal Knapsack
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = optimal solution / correct packing; CRIMSON = greedy suboptimality / half-left-behind
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no fractional relaxation proof, no DP knapsack, no (1-f) family proof. Wonder register.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Always grab the most valuable item per pound — and on one specific example you walk away with exactly half the gold, leaving the other half on the table. That 50% is the worst the strategy can ever do.

card.copy: Why Greedy Can Guarantee Half the Optimal Knapsack

beats.narration_text: The knapsack problem: items with values and weights, a bag with fixed capacity. Greedy strategy: always pick the highest value-per-weight item that still fits. Simple and fast. But suboptimal.

production_viz.label: Knapsack with items; value/weight ratio labels; greedy pick order; capacity bar; TEAL=greedy, CRIMSON=left out

beats.narration_text: Worst case for greedy: capacity 1000. Item A: value 502, weight 501, ratio about 1.002. Items B and C: value 500 each, weight 500 each. Greedy picks A first — best ratio. But then B and C each need 500 — too big. Greedy gets value 502. Optimal: B plus C equals 1000. Ratio: 502 over 1000, about 50%.

card.copy: Can greedy ever do worse than 50%?
card.sub: The two-choice rule guarantees it cannot

beats.narration_text: No — and here's why. Consider two choices: the greedy packing, or the single most valuable item. The two-choice rule takes the better of these.

production_viz.label: Fork: (1) greedy packing vs (2) single best item; take max; TEAL = winner branch

beats.narration_text: Claim: 2 times the maximum of greedy and best single item is at least the optimal value. The fractional relaxation — where items can be split — is at most the optimal. The greedy packing plus the best item that didn't fit together form a fractional solution worth at least the fractional optimum.

production_viz.label: Inequality: 2×max(greedy, best_item) ≥ fractional_OPT ≥ OPT; chain diagram; TEAL annotation

beats.narration_text: So the two-choice rule — compare greedy output to the single best item, take whichever is larger — always achieves at least 50% of optimal. In our example: maximum of 502 and 502 equals 502, which is at least 50% of 1000. Check.

production_viz.label: Example: max(502, 502)=502 ≥ 50% of 1000; 50% guarantee verified; TEAL guarantee line

beats.narration_text: If items are restricted to be at most f times capacity in weight, the guarantee improves. A stalling item — one that prevents us from adding more — is at most f times capacity, so the greedy packing plus the staller is at least 1-minus-f times optimal. At f equals 0.1: guarantee at least 90%.

production_viz.label: Size cap slider: f=1.0→50% guarantee; f=0.5→50%; f=0.1→90%; f=0.01→99%; TEAL improvement curve

beats.narration_text: Why not just use dynamic programming for the exact answer? DP knapsack is O(n times W) where W is capacity — polynomial only if W is small. For large capacities — say W equals 10^18 — DP is infeasible. Greedy with the two-choice enhancement is O(n log n) and gives a provable guarantee.

production_viz.label: DP: O(n×W), breaks at large W. Greedy: O(n log n), provable ≥50%. Comparison table; TEAL=greedy advantage

beats.narration_text: Approximation algorithms trade exactness for tractability. The knapsack greedy is a clean example: a simple, fast algorithm with a provable worst-case guarantee. The guarantee is not "usually good" — it holds on every possible input.

production_viz.label: Exactness vs tractability trade-off diagram; 50% guarantee = worst case on every input; TEAL=proven bound

beats.narration_text: Greedy by value over weight: at least 50% of optimal, always. Add the two-choice rule: 50% guaranteed. Restrict item size: higher guarantee. Exact DP: too slow for large capacities. Approximation algorithms trade exactness for tractability.

card.sub: A provable guarantee on every possible input

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
